agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who use this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — delivery records are regulated artifacts in the logistics domain with legal, tax, customs, and contractual retention obligations.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete audit trail of a delivery lifecycle: pickup, transit, proof of delivery, exception events, timestamps, and associated metadata. A regulated artifact in logistics."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long specific categories of data must be stored, driven by legal, regulatory, contractual, and business requirements — not by storage cost preferences."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory compliance. Often needed years after delivery."},{"term":"Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by B2B customers to satisfy their own regulatory obligations (tax, customs, audit). Destroying upstream data can break downstream compliance."},{"term":"Right to Erasure","definition":"GDPR Article 17 right, but subject to exceptions including legal obligations and defense of legal claims — which apply to delivery records."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The minimum period a business is legally required to retain specific records, which overrides any internal desire to delete them."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for the longest applicable statutory retention period across all jurisdictions the platform operates in (EU/UK).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting records before statutory minimums is a legal violation that exposes the company to fines, failed audits, and inability to defend legal claims. This is not a policy preference — it is a legal constraint.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record is auto-deleted before the maximum of all applicable statutory retention periods has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"UK/EU tax authorities require retention of transaction records (including delivery records that evidence taxable supply) for a minimum of 6 years (UK HMRC) or 10 years (some EU member states).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax record destruction before statutory minimums constitutes a criminal offense in most EU/UK jurisdictions. A 90-day policy would destroy tax evidence within the first quarter.","testable_assertion":"Delivery records associated with taxable transactions are retained for at least the jurisdiction-specific tax retention minimum (6-10 years depending on member state)."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer contracts that reference data availability for compliance reporting create contractual retention obligations that must be honored regardless of internal storage preferences.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying data that customers contractually depend on for their own compliance constitutes breach of contract and could expose B2B customers to their own regulatory violations.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record is deleted while any B2B customer contract requires its availability for compliance reporting."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be available for the duration of any potential dispute resolution or insurance claim window, typically 6 years (UK Limitation Act) for breach of contract claims.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without POD records, the company cannot defend against false non-delivery claims, chargebacks, or insurance disputes. This is existential risk.","testable_assertion":"POD records remain retrievable for at least 6 years from delivery date."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR does not require deletion of delivery records at 90 days. Article 17(3) provides explicit exceptions for legal obligations, defense of legal claims, and public interest archiving.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal interpretation that could evolve with case law, but the current position is well-established. GDPR is not a justification for 90-day deletion.","testable_assertion":"Data retention policy documentation explicitly cites Article 17(3) exceptions for delivery records and does not use GDPR as justification for premature deletion."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Customs and cross-border delivery records in the EU/UK must be retained per customs regulations (typically 3-7 years depending on jurisdiction and goods type).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customs authorities can audit historical shipments. Missing records result in fines and potential loss of customs broker privileges.","testable_assertion":"Cross-border delivery records are retained for the customs retention period of the strictest applicable jurisdiction."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record exists for a completed delivery in any EU/UK jurisdiction","when":"The auto-deletion process evaluates it at 90 days post-delivery","then":"The record MUST NOT be deleted because 90 days is below every known statutory retention minimum (tax: 6-10yr, limitation: 6yr, customs: 3-7yr)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"The platform serves B2B customers who use delivery data for compliance reporting","when":"Any data retention policy change is proposed","then":"All active B2B customer contracts must be reviewed for data availability clauses before any retention reduction is implemented"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery record is subject to multiple retention requirements (tax, customs, contractual, limitation period)","when":"Determining the retention period for that record","then":"The longest applicable retention period governs; the record is retained until ALL obligations have expired"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The engineering team wants to reduce storage costs","when":"Proposing changes to delivery record retention","then":"The approach must use tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) or archival compression rather than deletion to achieve cost savings without violating retention obligations"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A delivery record has passed ALL applicable statutory, contractual, and limitation retention periods","when":"The retention evaluation runs","then":"The record may be deleted or anonymized, and an audit log entry must record the deletion with the justification"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2B customer files a compliance audit request at day 89, referencing records that would be deleted at day 90","expected_behavior":"Records must be preserved. But more fundamentally, 90-day deletion means this scenario would occur constantly since B2B customers expect multi-year availability."},{"scenario":"A consumer disputes a delivery 2 years after the fact (within UK 6-year limitation period)","expected_behavior":"The delivery record and POD must be available to defend the claim. Under the 90-day policy, this evidence would have been destroyed 21 months ago."},{"scenario":"HMRC or EU tax authority audits transactions from 4 years ago","expected_behavior":"All delivery records evidencing taxable supply must be produced. Under 90-day policy, the company would face penalties for destroyed tax records."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery records needed for customs audit 3 years post-delivery","expected_behavior":"Records must be available. Failure to produce them risks customs penalties and loss of broker privileges."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim for damaged goods filed 18 months after delivery","expected_behavior":"Full delivery chain records including timestamps, handling events, and POD must be available to support or contest the claim."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"CATASTROPHIC. Tax penalties for destroyed records can reach 100% of tax owed plus criminal prosecution. B2B contract breach liability could be multi-million. Loss of ability to defend delivery disputes means absorbing all claims. Storage savings (thousands/year) are dwarfed by potential penalties (millions).","operational":"Destroys the company's ability to perform historical analysis, route optimization based on historical data, seasonal planning, and trend analysis. B2B customers would lose compliance capability, triggering mass contract terminations.","user":"B2B customers would face their own compliance failures when delivery data they depend on disappears. B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries for disputes, returns, or warranty claims. Trust destruction across both segments.","risk":"CRITICAL regulatory risk across multiple dimensions: tax law violation, customs violations, inability to defend legal claims, breach of B2B contracts, potential GDPR violation (deleting data you are legally required to retain is itself a compliance issue). The 7-year policy was likely set deliberately to cover the longest statutory requirement.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Cannot fulfill their own compliance reporting obligations. Would likely terminate contracts and potentially sue for damages caused by data destruction."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Lose ability to dispute deliveries, file insurance claims, or reference delivery history beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Finance/Tax Team","impact":"Cannot produce records for tax audits. Company faces penalties and potential criminal liability."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance Team","impact":"Cannot defend against claims or produce records for regulatory inquiries. Limitation period for contract claims is 6 years — they lose 5.75 years of defensibility."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Lose historical data for route optimization, capacity planning, and performance analysis."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect. This is a fundamental business rule change with legal consequences."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because critical legal and regulatory context was never provided: no legal review, no DPO consultation, no analysis of applicable retention statutes by jurisdiction, no review of B2B contract terms. However, the general direction is HIGH confidence — every indicator says 90 days is far too short. The specific retention periods cited are based on general EU/UK regulatory knowledge and would need jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal validation.","high_confidence_areas":["90 days is far below any known statutory retention minimum for delivery records in EU/UK","This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — it changes fundamental business compliance posture","B2B customers will be directly harmed","The current 7-year policy is almost certainly legally motivated","Tiered storage is the correct approach to reduce costs without violating retention"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per EU member state the platform operates in","Specific B2B contract clauses regarding data availability","Whether the platform handles customs/cross-border deliveries","Whether any legal review was conducted before this proposal","The specific GDPR lawful basis currently used for retaining delivery data"],"assumptions":["The platform has not obtained a legal opinion endorsing 90-day retention","B2B contracts include data availability provisions for compliance reporting","Delivery records include or are linked to tax-relevant transaction data","The current 7-year retention policy was established with legal guidance","The platform handles deliveries that cross customs boundaries within EU/UK"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review was provided or referenced. A data retention change in EU/UK logistics requires analysis of: (1) tax retention requirements per member state, (2) customs record retention requirements, (3) limitation periods for contractual and tortious claims, (4) GDPR lawful basis analysis, (5) DPO sign-off. None of this was provided. The proposal was framed as 'no business logic changes' which is factually incorrect — retention periods ARE business logic encoded as legal obligations.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","B2B Customers","Finance/Tax Team","Data Protection Officer","All end users"],"options":["REJECT the 90-day deletion proposal and require legal review before any retention changes","Pursue tiered storage (hot → warm → cold/archive) to reduce costs while maintaining full retention compliance","Commission a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention analysis before proposing any new retention periods"],"recommendation":"REJECT this proposal immediately. Do NOT implement 90-day auto-deletion under any circumstances. Instead, commission a legal retention analysis and pursue tiered storage architecture to achieve the legitimate goal of cost reduction without regulatory exposure. The engineering team's framing of this as 'no business logic changes' must be corrected — this is one of the highest-risk business logic changes possible."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about B2B customer contracts and their data availability terms. Reducing retention could constitute breach of contract across the entire B2B customer base simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Account Management","Legal Team"],"options":["Review all active B2B contracts for data retention and availability clauses","Engage B2B customers in advance of any retention changes","Maintain current retention for B2B-associated records regardless of other changes"],"recommendation":"Review all B2B contracts before any retention policy changes. Any change must be compatible with contractual obligations or renegotiated with affected customers first."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No Data Protection Officer (DPO) consultation referenced. Under GDPR, changes to data retention policies require DPO involvement. The DPO would also confirm that the GDPR Article 17(3) exceptions apply to delivery records, meaning deletion at 90 days is not required and may itself be a violation.","affected_stakeholders":["DPO","Compliance Team","All data subjects"],"options":["Require DPO sign-off on any retention policy change","Document GDPR lawful basis for current 7-year retention","Ensure any new policy is GDPR-compliant in both directions (not retaining too long AND not deleting too soon)"],"recommendation":"Engage the DPO immediately. No retention policy change should proceed without DPO review and documented GDPR analysis."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would destroy delivery records required by EU/UK tax law (6-10 year retention minimums)","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would breach B2B customer contracts that depend on delivery data for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion would eliminate Proof of Delivery records needed to defend legal claims within the 6-year limitation period","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"The proposal was characterized as 'no business logic changes' which is factually incorrect — retention periods are legally mandated business rules, not infrastructure configuration","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"No legal review, DPO consultation, or regulatory analysis was conducted before proposing this change","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-6"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who use this data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — delivery records are regulated artifacts in the logistics domain with legal, tax, customs, and contractual retention obligations.","bounded_context":"Deliver

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in EU/UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive business and regulatory implications.

Key issues:
- EU/UK have specific data retention requirements (GDPR, tax laws, customs regulations)
- B2B customers depend on this data for compliance reporting
- 90 days is extremely short for logistics records
- Nobody has mentioned legal/regulatory review

This is clearly a missing_context situation - critical regulatory and legal information was never provided by anyone in the scenario.

Let me produce the analysis.
